name: scientific-brainstorming
description: Creative research ideation and exploration. Use for open-ended brainstorming sessions, exploring interdisciplinary connections, challenging assumptions, or identifying research gaps. Best for early-stage research planning when you do not have specific observations yet. For formulating testable hypotheses from data use hypothesis-generation.
capabilities: Guides researchers through five-phase brainstorming workflow from context understanding to next steps
Applies structured methodologies like SCAMPER, Six Thinking Hats, TRIZ, and Biomimicry
Facilitates interdisciplinary connections by drawing parallels across scientific domains
Challenges assumptions and encourages unconventional research perspectives
Synthesizes ideas into concrete research directions and action items
author: K-Dense-AI
securitySummary: Documentation-only skill with no executable code. Static findings triggered on benign markdown text about brainstorming methodologies. No network calls, no external commands, no filesystem access. Safe for publication.
valueStatement: Scientists often struggle to generate novel research directions during early-stage planning. This skill guides creative scientific ideation through structured brainstorming methodologies, helping researchers explore interdisciplinary connections and identify hidden research opportunities.
limitations: Does not access external databases or live literature search capabilities
Cannot verify scientific validity of generated hypotheses through experimentation
Effectiveness depends on user providing sufficient research context
Not designed for hypothesis testing or data analysis tasks

useCases.title: Explore New Research Directions
useCases.description: Graduate students use the skill when stuck on thesis direction or seeking novel angles on established research questions.

useCases.title: Cross-Disciplinary Innovation
useCases.description: Established researchers apply techniques to bridge their domain with adjacent fields, discovering unexpected methodological connections.

useCases.title: Team Ideation Sessions
useCases.description: Research teams use structured techniques during planning meetings to ensure diverse perspectives and avoid groupthink.

promptTemplates.title: Initial Research Exploration
promptTemplates.prompt: I am working on [research area/topic]. I want to brainstorm some novel research directions. Let me describe my current work: [describe your research context, goals, and challenges]. Ask me questions to understand my domain, then help me explore creative new directions.

promptTemplates.title: Apply SCAMPER Framework
promptTemplates.prompt: I want to improve my current research approach using the SCAMPER framework. My method involves [describe your current methodology]. Walk me through each SCAMPER element and help me generate modified versions of my approach.

promptTemplates.title: Six Perspective Analysis
promptTemplates.prompt: I am evaluating whether to pursue [specific research idea]. Help me analyze this from all six perspectives of the Six Thinking Hats method. Guide me through White, Red, Black, Yellow, Green, and Blue hat thinking.

promptTemplates.title: Cross-Domain Innovation
promptTemplates.prompt: I work in [your field] and want to discover connections to [target field]. My current challenge is [describe the challenge]. What concepts, methods, or approaches from [target field] might offer new perspectives?

bestPractices: Maintain a conversational balance where the researcher does at least 50 percent of the talking and idea generation.
Push beyond obvious ideas by explicitly encouraging risky and unconventional research directions.
Adapt techniques to the researcher's thinking style rather than rigidly following predefined frameworks.
antiPatterns: Do not lecture the researcher or dominate the conversation with AI-generated ideas.
Avoid forcing specific brainstorming methods when the natural conversation flow is productive.
Do not let AI-generated ideas substitute for the researcher's domain expertise and judgment.

faq.question: What types of research questions work best with this skill?
faq.answer: This skill excels at early-stage research planning where the goal is generating novel directions. It works well for exploring interdisciplinary connections, improving existing methodologies, and identifying research gaps.

faq.question: Can this skill help with interdisciplinary projects?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill explicitly draws parallels across scientific domains and uses techniques like Biomimicry and cross-domain analogies to connect ideas from different fields.

faq.question: How long should a brainstorming session last?
faq.answer: A productive session typically runs 30 to 60 minutes. The skill guides researchers through five phases: context understanding, divergent exploration, connection making, critical evaluation, and synthesis of next steps.

faq.question: Does this skill work with all Claude models?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill is compatible with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. The conversational brainstorming approach works across all models with varying levels of creative exploration.

faq.question: How do I integrate this with my existing research workflow?
faq.answer: Use the skill during planning phases before experiments begin. The output of each session should be concrete next steps such as literature searches, pilot experiments, or collaboration opportunities.

faq.question: When should I not use this skill?
faq.answer: Avoid this skill when you already have specific hypotheses to test or when you need data analysis. For hypothesis formulation from existing data, consider using the hypothesis-generation skill instead.
